Name: Jahmyr Gibbs
Outlook: Perhaps our small brains cannot comprehend this man’s greatness in fantasy football. Gibbs is a superstar, totaling the most TDs (49) in NFL history by any RB through their first three years in the NFL. After Dan Campbell took over as play-caller (Week 10), Gibbs had the seventh most receptions of any player in the NFL as David Montgomery was phased out of the offense. Gibbs should see the biggest workload of his career and is a worthy selection at 1.01 this year. Consider your opponent’s pants pee-stained every time he touches the ball.
Dynasty: Expect a huge bag of money thrown his way over the summer. He is one of the most explosive players in the league and a surefire early first-round pick in startups. If you want to take him ahead of Bijan or one of the elite WRs, we do not blame you.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Bijan Robinson
Outlook: It was a video game-like 2025 season for Bijan, totaling 2,298 yards from scrimmage, the most since CMC in 2019. He accounted for 41% of Atlanta’s total team yardage thanks to ranking first among all RBs in forced missed tackles and percentage of the team’s first-read targets. If you are splitting hairs at the top of your drafts, the only knock on Bijan might be the functionality of the Atlanta offense and how the QB position shakes out. He is all that is great in fantasy football and a true building block if you have the pleasure of drafting him.
Dynasty: He is arguably the best offensive player in football, and he enters this offseason likely up for a major contract extension. Bijan should once again be featured in 2026 under new offensive play-callers Kevin Stefanski and Tommy Rees.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Christian McCaffrey
Outlook: This is the stuff fantasy dreams are made of. If you called your shot with CMC in 2025, oh baby, were you living large while the rest of your league mates cowered in fear due to “he’s gonna get injured” fears. The RB1 was a consistency monster as essentially two players in one for fantasy. Using only his receiving stats, he would have been the WR11 in half-point leagues or the WR8 in full PPR. He tied Amon-Ra St. Brown for the league lead in red zone receptions while totaling a whopping 413 touches, the most by a fantasy RB since DeMarco Murray in 2014. Whew! That type of workload suggests some regression is coming for the 30-year-old, but we still have him ranked as a top-5 RB. He is him, and his skill set for fantasy is like none other since the LaDanian Tomlinson days.
Dynasty: CMC is an all-timer for fantasy football. If you have ever had the opportunity to experience and inhabit a CMC #1 overall season, you likely won your league. However, we are not here to pat your back, but instead offer a word of caution. Since 2020, 11 RBs saw 330+ touches in a regular season; ALL of them saw a decrease of 45+ touches the following year, and only two played a full season. He can still be awesome for 2026, but we would advise shopping him in your league to get an absolute haul. This is “get paid” advice. 30-year-old RBs coming off this big of a workload have a tough time repeating.

Name: Jonathan Taylor
Outlook: Oh man, those first 10 weeks of JTT were legendary! His five 30+ fantasy point games were tied with 2000 Marshall Faulk and 2006 LaDanian Tomlinson for the most ever through the first ten weeks of a season. As a receiver, he was more involved than ever, setting career highs in targets, receptions, and receiving yards. Unfortunately, the Colts’ offense tanked down the stretch with Philip Rivers & Riley Leonard under center. Taylor’s production essentially was cut in half, nosediving from 23.3 fantasy points per game with Daniel Jones to 11.8 over those final five games. JTT is still a locked-in RB1 for us, although the first month of the season is a brutal stretch for the Colts.
Dynasty: In dynasty, this is the time if you aren’t a contender to trade the 27-year-old back for a haul. Taylor is in his final year under contract, but the Indianapolis front office could clean house this offseason if Shane Steichen fails to make the playoffs.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Puka Nacua
Outlook: Puka was dominant in 2025 with a league-leading 107 receiving yards per game and six weeks of 20+ fantasy points, the most among WRs. He was an absolute dominator on 50/50 balls, tracking down 27 contested catches, the most of any WR over the last decade. Aside from off-the-field issues, he is simply unstoppable and off-the-charts in every per-route metric we care about for fantasy. If you wanted to take Puka at the 1.01 in a full PPR league, we totally understand.
Dynasty: Money please! That sound is the giant bag of cash coming Puka’s way after he totaled the second-most receiving yards through a WR’s first three years in the NFL behind only Justin Jefferson. We have him firmly amongst an elite three of WRs we would take at the very beginning of dynasty startups.

Name: Ja'Marr Chase
Outlook: Let us hit you with some hard-hitting analysis. Ja’Marr Chase is good at the game! Even with Joe Burrow missing a huge chunk of the season, the dude still finished as the WR4 with over 15 fantasy points per game. Finishing as the WR1 overall is obviously in play, especially if Burrow can finally stay healthy and have one of those elite TD seasons. He is well worth the pick early in Round 1 of fantasy drafts.
Dynasty: In our opinion, Chase is still viewed as one of those few untouchables in dynasty formats. Despite already being in the league for five years, he is still just 26 years old and at the peak of his prime. We have him locked in as a no-brainer top-five overall asset in dynasty.

Name: James Cook III
Outlook: After agreeing to a four-year contract extension in mid-August, James Cook punched every "TD-regression" metric in the face in 2025, leading the league in rushing yards and finishing as a top-6 fantasy back. He paced the league with 11 games of 80+ yards rushing and ranked 3rd in yards after contact. The only stain on Cook’s resume is his declining involvement in the passing game, as he averaged just 1.9 receptions per game last year. Fantasy managers can have confidence in the continuity of this Buffalo offense, the play-calling, and an offensive line that continues to maul opposing fronts. Cook remains a strong RB1 for us in 2026.
Dynasty: Over the last two seasons, his 28 total rushing TDs rank fourth among all RBs. He is tailor-made for Joe Brady's zone scheme and is under contract until 2030. At just 26 years old and tied to Josh Allen, he remains a steady dynasty asset as a low-end RB1.

Name: Jaxon Smith-Njigba
Outlook: If you grabbed JSN in the third round last year, you likely won your league as Smith-Njigba was incredible all year long, leading the league in receiving with almost 1,800 yards and 10 scores. His efficiency could regress after he averaged a silly 3.74 yards per route run, but he is still an easy click in the first round of fantasy drafts as one of the few guys in the league who can command a 30+% target share.
Dynasty: Seattle just made JSN one of the highest-paid WRs in the league, and we know the talent is immense. He is an easy top-five WR moving forward and well worth a first-round selection in start up drafts.

Name: De'Von Achane
Outlook: Coming off one of the most consistent fantasy seasons for an RB of all-time, hopefully, any doubts you had about Achane should be buried deep in the ground. He became one of four RBs ever to score 11+ fantasy points in every single game and ranked #1 in yards per carry (5.7) and explosive rush rate (10%) among all qualifying RBs. When you mix in his receiving work, he is a cheat code at the position. Despite Miami’s low win expectation for 2026, we are still in on his price tag at the 1/2 turn in draft as a locked-in RB1.
Dynasty: Achane signed a four-year, $68M extension, making him one of the highest-paid RBs in the league. While the Dolphins might be going through a rebuild, his dynasty value remains stable as a top-5 RB in startups. The pass-catching cheat code that he is will help him age well in this format.

Name: Ashton Jeanty
Outlook: Let’s be clear: Jeanty checks nearly every box we want in fantasy. Jeanty was actually Jason’s ‘Bust’ pick last year on the show, relative to ADP. It is not because we all didn’t love the talent and draft capital, but it is hard to be a fantasy difference maker at the RB position when your surroundings are as bad as they were a season ago. The O-line was a disaster, Pete Carroll ran a completely unserious operation, and Geno Smith regressed massively. It led to Las Vegas ranking dead last in TDs per game. Again, it is hard to be a great fantasy RB when that’s the case. Fast forward one year, and suddenly, we are more optimistic about where the Raiders are headed. Sure, Fernando Mendoza is a bit of a question mark, but the team put significant investment in the O-line, and we are big fans of Klint Kubiak’s scheme. Look for Jeanty to make a big-time jump in his efficiency metrics in Year 2, and if the Raiders offense can just be around league average, he could push for a top-8 RB season. Beyond the 29 rushing TDs last year as a Heisman runner-up, he is also one of three first-round RBs to see 20+ of their team’s receiving yardage in a single season (2023), joining some guys named Christian McCaffrey and Jahmyr Gibbs. The Raiders certainly were smitten, taking him higher than any RB in the NFL draft since Saquon Barkley in 2018. If you possess this type of draft capital (sixth overall) and elite college production at this young of an age (21 years old), you crush for fantasy. Expect a mammoth workload on a much-improved Raiders team. We have him as a fringe first-round pick in redraft, but certainly understand if you want to call your shot ahead of other established backs.
Dynasty: The only RBs above Jeanty in our consensus start-up rankings are Jahmyr Gibbs and Bijan Robinson. That tells you all you need to know about Jeanty’s future in dynasty leagues. As a prospect, he was one of those rare talents with top-10 draft capital, so you can feel pretty confident you will get multiple high-end fantasy seasons from Ashton Jeanty.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Amon-Ra St. Brown
Outlook: St. Brown is the pinnacle of consistency. He has now finished as a top-8 fantasy WR in four straight seasons, and he has banked the WR3 overall finish in three straight years as Jared Goff’s high-volume WR1. There aren’t many WRs we would want over him in redraft leagues this season, making him a rock-solid pick in Round 1 of drafts.
Dynasty: St. Brown is locked in with the Lions through the 2028 season, and he is still just 26 years old. We expect the fantasy goodness to continue for the next two to three years at a minimum, making him one of the most valuable WRs in all of dynasty.

Name: Derrick Henry
Outlook: You can tell your grandkids you lived through the glory years known as "Derrick Henry SZN". He is an absolute fantasy legend finishing 2025 with his eighth straight season of double-digit rushing TDs. He saw the most carries inside the 5-yard line of his career and ranked third among all RBs in yards per carry. “Father Time” is usually undefeated when it comes to 30+ year old RBs, but Henry has kicked him outside the wrestling ring the last couple of years. Our projections still have him ranked as a low-end RB1 with a slight deduction in full PPR leagues.
Dynasty: The future Hall of Famer is under contract for two more years as he carries a team-friendly $5.5 million cap hit in 2026 that balloons to $16.2 in 2027.  It is worth noting that since 2010, only one 32+ year old RB has had a double-digit TD season: DeAngelo Williams (PIT) in 2015 had 11 rushing TDs. If you have Henry in dynasty, you could entertain offers or just ride this man into the sunset!

Name: CeeDee Lamb
Outlook: Lamb had a good season in 2025, but it didn’t feel great considering you likely had to take him in the first round last year. He missed a few games because of the high ankle sprain, and he only scored three TDs. In the game we play, that is just not going to get it done. However, he is coming off a career high 14.4 yards per reception and has now finished with 1,000+ yards in five straight seasons. If that TD variance swings back in his direction, CeeDee can absolutely finish as a top-5 WR in 2026.
Dynasty: Lamb remains one of the most valuable WRs in all of dynasty, but it is at least worth considering that he is 27 years old. He will remain ultra-productive for the next couple of years, so if you have him, keep taking that top-tier production. Just have it on your radar for the next two years that the trade window might close more quickly than you think. We aren’t saying he will fall off any time soon, but we generally prefer to be a year early in order to maximize returns in the trade market.

Name: Kenneth Walker III
Outlook: Ken Bone! Walker was a key piece of the Seattle offense down the stretch and parlayed his Super Bowl MVP award by receiving a big old bag of money from the Chiefs. He signed a three-year deal worth just over $14M per year. He is still only 25 years old and remains one of the best home run hitters in the game, something that was severely lacking in KC's run game over the last two years. For context, over the last two years, Chiefs RBs have run for 10+ yards less than 9% of the time, 31st amongst 32 teams. Enter Walker, who was third in the league last season in rate of 10+ yard runs and #1 in forced missed tackle rate. Expect 18-20 touches per game as the team re-establishes the run game and the red zone approach under OC Eric Bienemy en route to RB1 status for the Bone Zone.
Dynasty: If you have Ken Walker on your dyno squad, you have our permission to join the Hitman in removing your pants at this time. This was an incredible run out for a player who always tantalized with his per-touch ability. Only 25 years old, he is a top-10 dynasty RB in startups for us.

Name: Omarion Hampton
Outlook: It was a frustrating rookie season for Omarion Hampton, who missed Weeks 6-13 with a small fracture in his ankle. However, when he was on the field, you saw flashes of what he can do in fantasy. He only appeared in nine games, but in those contests, Hampton handled a 30% share of the team’s total rush attempts and targets. For context, anything at or above 30% in that metric is elite and quite rare. Now entering Year 2 in the league, there is room for that number to grow, and if it does in this Mike McDaniel scheme with a healthier offensive line, Hampton is a strong candidate to finish inside the top 10 at the position.
Dynasty: We are not ready to put Hampton up there with guys like Bijan and Gibbs, but Hampton is a surefire RB1 in dynasty startups. He is only 23 years old and has that first-round draft capital that suggests he will have multiple seasons as LA’s bell cow. If you have him, ride this thing out for the next two to three years, knowing you have one of dynasty’s most valuable RBs.

Name: Chase Brown
Outlook: It looked like Brown might end up one of 2025’s biggest fantasy busts after averaging just 8.1 fantasy points per game on 51 total yards through the first five weeks of the season. He ended up becoming a league winner en route to seeing the fourth-most targets among all RBs while averaging 100 total yards per game from Week 6 on. The only yellow flag in his profile is his lack of major success inside the 5-yard line. Cincinnati failed to bring in any other significant RB competition, making Brown yet again a candidate to finish as an RB1 for the third year in a row as part of this Joe Burrow-led offense.
Dynasty: Brown survived yet another off-season where the Bengals failed to add a challenger in the RB room. As a PPR monster attached to a high-powered, Brown is in a sweet spot for fantasy as we consider him a fringe top-10 dynasty RB. Expect a potential off-season extension to surface this summer.

Name: Saquon Barkley
Outlook: Coming off a legendary 2024 campaign and Super Bowl run, there were warning signs about regression coming across the board for Saquon and the Eagles' offense. He bottomed out in a number of areas, including plummeting from 17 runs of 20+ yards to only four in 2025. He also shockingly converted only one of his 13 carries inside the 5-yard line into a TD, but still volumed his way to finishing as the RB13. With an offensive coordinator change, we expect Saquon to bounce back in 2026 at a discounted price in the second round of your drafts. Your league mates might bring up the fact he was a “bust,” but we are willing to forgive and forget.
Dynasty: Entering his age-29 season, this is the time to make a decision on Saquon based on your team’s competitive window. If you are a contender, you can expect one more year of elite production, but rebuilds should consider shopping him for at least a first-round pick and another young piece. He is under contract for two more seasons, but we are not valuing him as an elite asset beyond 2026.

Name: Josh Jacobs
Outlook: It looked like Jacobs was going to repeat as a certifiable RB1 for fantasy, averaging 22 opportunities and 1.2 rushing TDs per game through the first ten weeks of the season. He hit a major wall down the stretch, dealing with a knee injury and averaging just 10.5 fantasy points from Week 13 on. We know what he is: a TD monster with a league-leading 24 rushing TDs inside the 10-yard line over the last two seasons. The workload should be there, but we are slightly lower than where he is being drafted, viewing the aging RB as more of a fringe top-12 guy.
Dynasty: While his contract says he has two more years left in Lambeau, there is language that would allow the Packers to get out after this year if he falters. At age 28, he fits the bill as someone moving along the age curve, and it is better to get out ahead of his dynasty lifecycle.

Name: Justin Jefferson
Outlook: JJetts disappointed fantasy managers who grabbed him early in drafts last season, but was it really his fault? I mean, I think we all know who to blame here…“Nine” was one of the worst starting QBs in football, and you may forget, but Carson Wentz (yes, that guy) started five games while Max Brosmer attempted to play football in the NFL. Spoiler: It did not go well. Basically, we are completely ignoring Jefferson’s 2025 season. He is still one of the best in the game, and while Kyler is not a top-five QB, he is certainly an upgrade on whatever the heck the Vikings were doing in 2025. He is one of fantasy’s most obvious bounce-back players for 2026 and is well worth a look in Round 1 of drafts.
Dynasty: Jefferson is still one of the most valuable WRs in dynasty formats despite his down 2025 campaign, and we are confidently taking him in the first round of startup drafts.

Name: Kyren Williams
Outlook: Ever since Sean McVay gave Kyren the reins, he has been amazing for fantasy, finishing as a top-10 back in three straight seasons. He is rarely hitting big plays or flashing elite speed in the open field, but he is as dependable as they come, and McVay trusts this dude. Blake Corum is probably going to steal a few TDs and work in for a handful of touches per game, but when the game is on the line, McVay is calling Kyren’s number. In 2026, he is projected as a volume-based high-end RB2 who could bank another RB1 finish if the TDs go his way.
Dynasty: Williams signed a three-year extension with the Rams, but fantasy managers should really treat this like a two-year window. He is already entering his age-26 season.

Name: Drake London
Outlook: Can we get this man a QB? We know London is one of the most talented WRs in the league, yet it feels like there is still so much untapped potential given what has been going on in Atlanta. Even though Tua and Penix have their warts, we are closing our eyes in drafts and betting on talent with Drake, who has been targeted on 30% of his routes over the last two seasons. In case you missed it, the Falcons are trotting out some combination of the three stooges behind London: Jahan Dotson, Zachariah Branch, and Olamide Zacchaeus. Let’s get this guy 10+ targets a game and see what happens!
Dynasty: In dynasty, betting on young, highly drafted, and productive WRs is a winning strategy long term. That is why we will continue to bet on London even if the surrounding environment leaves a lot to be desired. Let’s hope the Falcons can find their long-term franchise QB next offseason because the Drake is here to stay. He signed a massive 4-year extension with the team in early June, making him one of the highest paid WRs in the league.

Name: Jeremiyah Love
Outlook: The Notre Dame superstar became the highest draft RB since Saquon Barkley after the Cardinals selected him third overall. His combination of size, speed, early breakout age (19.3), and production is a recipe for success at the next level. However, with Tyler Allgeier and James Conner on the roster as well as a bevy of legit pass-catchers, we might need to temper expectations for fantasy. His weekly workload will be in question despite the fact that RBs drafted inside the top 10 have averaged 19+ touches per game in Year 1 over the last decade. We have Love ranked as an RB2 for 2026.

Name: Cam Skattebo
Outlook: How good was the Skatt man for the 10 weeks of the season? He was the RB10, averaging 14.5 fantasy points per game, including a month’s stretch where the Giants fed him the ball 22+ times a game. His gruesome ankle fracture was tough to watch, as his energy and enthusiasm made him one of our favorite players to watch in 2025. He came out saying he would be ready for Week 1, but we would expect his best football to be played in the back half of the season, as it may take time to regain his explosiveness and dynamic ability. Consider him an RB2 for your fantasy squads on a run-heavy Giants team with an improved offensive line and a new scheme.
Dynasty: Before going down to injury, he ripped off six straight top-24 weekly finishes among RBs, so he clearly has the skillset to succeed in the game we play. That said, whenever there is a regime change, the dynasty value for Day 3 RBs is always a bit murky. For now, he is the clear best fantasy option in the backfield and a mid-RB2 in our startup rankings.

Name: Brock Bowers
Outlook: Who has two thumbs and might be the only legitimate pass-catching option on his team in 2026? This guy! Bowers followed up his incredible TE1 rookie season in 2024 with a TE8 finish in 2025, which, honestly, feels almost just as successful given the state of the QB and organization in Vegas. His targets per game dropped from 9.0 to 7.1, but he broke through with seven TDs in only 12 games played. Add in some competent QB play, either in the form of Kirk Cousins or Fernando Mendoza, and Bowers could rival Trey McBride’s 169 targets of last year. There is so little target competition in Las Vegas that he could break fantasy in 2026.
Dynasty: With the revamping of the Las Vegas offense and Klint Kubiak’s arrival, is it possible that Brock Bowers has gained even more steam as the TE1 in dynasty? There will always be the Bowers/McBride debate, but regardless of which side you land on, Bowers can and should be the cornerstone of your dynasty squad if you are fortunate to have him.

Name: Nico Collins
Outlook: Even with C.J. Stroud disappointing more often than not from a real-life NFL perspective, Collins continues to post WR1 numbers on a per-game basis. He is just that dude in this offense, and with Tank Dell recovering from a major injury and last year’s rookies, Jayden Higgins and Jaylin Noel, unproven commodities, Collins’ elite target share remains locked in. He is on the short list of players who are capable of finishing as a top-five fantasy WR in 2026.

If you are a contender, you are obviously riding this out and taking the production, but if you are a rebuilding team, we do not mind putting him on the trade block and getting a haul. Our philosophy in dynasty is that we would generally prefer to be a year early rather than a year late.

Name: A.J. Brown

As for the player himself, last year probably felt frustrating as Philly’s offense sputtered and he finished with career lows in yards per reception (12.9). Even in a “down” year, Brown still finished as fantasy’s WR10 and an efficient 2+ yards per route run for his 7th consecutive season. Name: Chris Olave
Outlook: Olave was buried in ADP a year ago due to concerns about the Saints' QB room and his concussion history. Fantasy drafters who took the plunge on Olave in the middle rounds last season were rewarded in a big way as Olave banked career highs in targets (156), receptions (100), yards (1,163), and TDs (9) en route to a WR6 finish. Shough for the win! After Spencer Rattler was benched, Olave went on a tear, averaging 15.8 points per game with Tyler Shough under center. The target competition will be more difficult with the addition of Jordyn Tyson, but Olave is one of the most talented WRs in the league, and we love Kellen Moore’s up-tempo scheme. We would not be shocked to see Olave post back-to-back WR1 seasons.
Dynasty: Olave should be able to cash in a huge payday from the Saints in the very near future, as he is entering the final year of his rookie deal after New Orleans picked up his fifth-year option. With the Saints trying to build around Tyler Shough on a cheap rookie contract, Olave’s extension should come sooner rather than later, giving him stability as a fringe WR1 in dynasty start-ups.

Name: George Pickens

His stats are a bit juiced for those games without CeeDee Lamb, but the point remains - Pickens is a super talented WR who plays in a scheme we like with a QB in Dak who can support elite fantasy seasons. Does he end up getting a huge extension with Dallas, or does he go chase the bag elsewhere in 2027? Name: Trey McBride
Outlook: If you were able to behold the TE season to end all TE seasons, you are probably still singing the praises of TMcB. With Jacoby Brissett at the helm, Trey McBride was unlocked, running 696(!) routes while breaking the single-season TE reception record with 126. He took advantage of various injuries among other Cardinals pass catchers to finish the year with 11 receiving TDs, a massive jump from the two he scored the year previous. We know that the TD total stands quite a chance to regress on a Cardinals team projected to win the fewest games in the NFL. Name: Breece Hall
Outlook: After signing a three-year extension following a tumultuous offseason, it is clear Breece remains the Jets’ main guy in the backfield. The opportunities to score were slim in 2025 as the Jets were tied or trailing on 93% of their offensive snaps, the worst rate in the league. Zoom out a bit further, and you will see that the team ranked dead last in rush attempts inside the 5-yard line for the third year in a row. His volume and breakaway run rate can make up for the team’s shortcomings. With a 45+ reception floor, Hall can be a valuable RB2 and potentially finish as an RB1 if the TDs finally fall in his favor.

One of the most productive and efficient backs over the last three seasons, the Jets rewarded Breece with a brand new three-year deal worth just over $15 million per year. Some dynasty managers out there may have been hoping for Breece to finally be unleashed on a new team and hopefully a better offense, but this contract says he will get plenty of volume in New York.

Name: Bucky Irving
Outlook: While the majority of 2025 fantasy RBs stayed healthy, Irving dealt with foot and shoulder injuries, limiting him to just 10 games. He was extremely ineffective, ranking 51st in rush success rate and 47th in explosive rush rate among 55 qualifying RBs. The Buccaneers also failed to give him a single carry inside the 5-yard line all season! We still believe in the talent, but Irving is shaping up to be one of the bigger wildcard picks in the early rounds. Kenneth Gainwell will certainly steal passing downs work, and Sean Tucker was re-signed to be the Bucs’ red-zone hammer. If you still are bullish on Bucky, shoot your shot as your team’s RB2 in hopes the entire offense resets in 2026.
Dynasty: From Day 3 pick to dynasty darling, Bucky’s rough 2025 campaign can be chalked up to injuries, a new play-caller, and a spell of unfortunate TD luck. We have him firmly ranked as a high-end RB2 in dynasty startups, given his age (24 years old) and the game-breaking talent he displayed as a rookie.

Name: Javonte Williams
Outlook: One of the best fantasy stories of 2025 had to be the resurgence of Javonte Williams as Dallas' RB1. Among qualified RBs, Javonte was fourth in yards after contact per attempt while seeing 19 opportunities per game. For the first four years of his frustrating career, he had eight total TDs inside the 5-yard line; in 2025, he had 10! With no direct competition for touches, Javonte is a solid RB2 as part of a Dallas offense we love buying into for fantasy.
Dynasty: We did it! The Javonte hive that held strong after years of disappointment had its breakthrough in dynasty.  After a bounce-back season, Dallas rewarded him with a three-year extension that includes $16 million in guaranteed money. His receiving efficiency was puzzling as he totaled a putrid 137 receiving yards on 51 targets, the fewest of any player with 50+ targets over the last 40 years! Expect at least two years of legitimate production.

Name: Tetairoa McMillan
Outlook: McMillan delivered on the promise of being a top-10 NFL Draft pick a year ago, finishing with just over 1,000 yards and seven scores. The draft capital, Year 1 production, and efficiency marks suggest McMillan is poised for another step forward in his sophomore campaign. After seeing 25.4% of the target share a year ago as a rookie, T Mac is on our short list of WRs who could push for upwards of a 30% market share. With his talent, that should mean great things for fantasy.
Dynasty: It is all there for McMillan to be one of the more valuable WRs in dynasty leagues moving forward. He broke out as a rookie, has elite draft capital, and has very little target competition on his own team. He is looking like a young building block for the foreseeable future, as we all have him inside our top-12 at the position.

Name: Garrett Wilson
Outlook: All this dude does is earn targets at an elite level. Since 2023, G Willy’s targets per route run rates are 25%, 25%, and 26%. Simply put, he is one of the best in the game at commanding a high target share. Unfortunately, that hasn't always translated into elite fantasy seasons, but we attribute that to lackluster QB play compared to other NFL WR1s. *Geno Smith has entered the chat.* Sadly, it could be the exact same scenario for Wilson in 2026. The talent is there, and the targets will be there, but the offensive environment could hold him back for fantasy once again. Nevertheless, he is a rock-solid WR2 for your team and gets a boost in full PPR formats.
Dynasty: If we can get this man a real QB, he could break fantasy. Until that problem is solved (maybe in 2027?!), his name will probably hold more value on your dynasty squad than the actual fantasy production. He is entering his age-26 season, and he signed a massive $130 million extension not that long ago, tying him to New York through the 2030 season.

Name: Quinshon Judkins
Outlook: Judkins was everything the Browns envisioned when they selected him 34th overall in last year’s draft. Despite the team’s lack of success, he was a workhorse, averaging 18.3 touches per game and 34% of the team's total rush attempts and targets before a brutal dislocated ankle and fractured fibula ended his season in Week 16. He has a chance to be cleared for Week 1, though he will not be 100% when the season begins. Expect his production to pick up in the second half of the season, making him an awkward selection in the middle rounds of your draft.
Dynasty: He is expected to make a full recovery for the 2026 season and should be viewed as a top-12 dynasty RB even on a bad football team. He has the size and three-down skillset to be an elite performer for the next handful of years.

Name: Emeka Egbuka
Outlook: Respectfully, Gronk. What the heck, dude?! Before Gronk called his man Ee-Meek-Ah Eg-Big-Boo, Egbuka was on fire, operating as the WR1 in Tampa en route to a true rookie year breakout. After Gronk’s snafu on live TV, Egbuka fell off the map, averaging just 3.2 catches and 41.1 yards per game from Week 6 on. If you zoom out, however, there are a lot of reasons for that - Egbuka played through a minor hammy injury, Baker got banged up, the O-line was injured, and Emeka’s catchable target rate was terrible. If drafts present an opportunity to buy back in on the former first-round pick, we are ready to pounce, especially with Mike Evans now in San Francisco and Chris Godwin another year older.

He is young, he is a former first-round pick who averaged 1.75 yards per route run, and he showed he can play in this league. Godwin may not be on this team much longer, so if there is any small buying window in your league on Egbuka, thanks to his up-and-down rookie year, we are definitely interested in seeing what it costs to get him on your dyno squad.

Name: DeVonta Smith
Outlook: Smitty has always been a great talent at the WR position, but his fantasy value has been held in check with AJ Brown on the roster. With Brown gone, expect "Slim Reaper" to be one of the hottest names in fantasy. Smith will have a chance to set career highs in targets, receptions, and yards as the Eagles’ new WR1. His ADP will probably skyrocket, but the excitement is warranted for a guy who’s averaged 14.5 PPR points per game without Brown in the lineup going back to 2022.
Dynasty: Smith has always had the talent to produce like a WR1, but he has been pigeon-holed as the second option in Philly ever since the Eagles traded for AJ Brown. That should change in a big way in 2026. Expect Smith’s dynasty value to skyrocket, but just be aware that he is already turning 28 years old later this year. He is still in his prime for now, but the window is probably closing faster than you might realize.

Name: Tee Higgins
Outlook: Blurb- “Meat Higgins,” as he is known around Fantasy Footballers HQ, has now finished as a top-24 fantasy WR in four of the last five years as the Robin to Ja’Marr Chase’s Batman. As long as Joe Burrow stays healthy, we expect both guys to eat this year, with Higgins the more volatile option of the two Cincy WRs. We are fine with Higgins as a pick where he goes as a WR2 for your squad, but he has always got that sneaky WR1 upside if Chase happens to miss time.
Dynasty: Higgins still has three more years left on the contract extension that he signed before the 2025 season, and he is still just 27 years old. He likely has at least two to three more great seasons in him before we talk about a potential decline. Keep riding this thing out, especially if you are a contender.

Name: Rashee Rice
Outlook: Welp! Another offseason discussing Rashee Rice and his off-the-field issues. Rice missed the first six weeks of the 2025 season while serving a suspension, and this past offseason, he violated his probation when he tested positive for marijuana. As a result, he is serving jail time in June and July. On top of that, we also got news in late May that Rice underwent a “clean up” procedure in his knee that could sideline him for roughly two months. If you recall, Rice’s 2024 season came to an end after he suffered a season-ending LCL, hamstring and posterolateral corner injury. So, to recap, we have another legal issue and a health concern. It all adds up to make Rice a highly volatile player entering training camp, assuming he is cleared. When on the field, he produces like a WR1 for fantasy, but there are obviously concerns. We will be tracking his situation closely over the next few months.
Dynasty: If you are a Rice manager in dynasty, this latest off-the-field incident feels like a complete gut punch. He is so valuable in terms of his fantasy production, but he is in a contract year and likely losing trust with the Chiefs. If they aren’t willing to pay him, and he leaves the Andy Reid and Pat Mahomes scheme, does his future production take a hit, and what happens if he is in the news again for legal troubles? His dynasty value could take a massive hit in the next 12 months.

Name: D'Andre Swift
Outlook: Over the last six years, only two RBs finished inside the top 24 at the position every year: Derrick Henry and D'Andre Swift. Yep, it isn’t always the sexiest outcomes, but Swift is the clear lead back for a Chicago offense we want pieces of in fantasy. He set career highs in rushing yards, rushing TDs, and rush success rate behind a much- improved Bears offensive line. Over the back half of the season, he did cede work to Kyle Monangai, but we still view Swift as the lead guy in a 60-40 backfield. Does he make it seven years in a row as an RB2? Our projections certainly think so.
Dynasty: The 27-year-old has one more year under contract, but dynasty managers could look to flip him to RB-needy teams if they are looking to profit.

Name: TreVeyon Henderson
Outlook: Stop if you have heard this before: we love us some Hendo. It was a painfully slow start to his career, but he broke out in the second half as the RB8 from Week 9 on, averaging 16.7 fantasy points per game. When Rhamondre Stevenson was out for a month, he showcased bell cow capabilities, averaging 20+ opportunities per game and an 80% snap rate. We know he is explosive, but will the coaching staff trust him as the team’s main passing-downs back in 2026? He carries massive upside at his ADP, but we would recommend building out RB depth on your roster around him as you navigate the beginning of the season with Hendo.

We have him ranked as a top-15 RB in dynasty formats, but our projections might have him end up finishing lower for 2026.

Name: Travis Etienne Jr.
Outlook: After Jacksonville’s Week 8 bye, Etienne turned into a fantasy force, averaging 17.5 fantasy points per game as the RB7. He was a wild outlier as a pass-catcher, becoming the third RB over the last 25 years to score 6+ rec TDs on fewer than 40 receptions. The main headline for the man formerly known as Travis Etienne Jr. was the four-year free agent deal he signed to come home to New Orleans and be crowned Travis “Aychane” Jr. With Alvin Kamara phasing out of the team’s plans, Etienne will be given every opportunity to be a workhorse for this Kellen Moore-designed offense. While the Saint’s weekly TD expectation might be low, he should end up a solid RB2 on volume alone.
Dynasty: The finer details of Etienne’s deal need to be discussed, as his four-year contract sneakily is a “two-year” deal, as he carries a $0 dead cap hit in 2028. In the short term, the 27-year-old should hold RB2 value as the main workhorse for the Saints’ running game. We are not banking on anything of significant value after these first two years of the contract.

Name: Jameson Williams
Outlook: You know what is fun? Jamo ripping off chunk gains thanks to some of that elite speed. Dan Campbell knows this. John Morton? Not so much. Before Guns Mahoney took over play calling, Jameson Williams’ role was frustrating for fantasy as he was used as a low-volume, high aDOT boom/bust type of guy. However, after Campbell took over the offense, Jameson Williams was one of fantasy’s most valuable WRs as he was on pace for 1,400 yards after the Week 8 bye. It is important to note that Sam LaPorta was also out for a big chunk of the season. Still, the point remains: Jamo is crucial to the Lions’ offense reaching its full potential, and he should continue to have a high upside role that is worth targeting in fantasy drafts.
Dynasty: Williams just signed a massive three-year extension with the Lions, so he is locked in with Detroit through 2029. It is possible he never turns into a consistent WR1 for fantasy, but he is an extremely valuable dynasty asset who can win you a week when those big plays hit.

Name: Christian Watson
Outlook: Watson’s bug-a-boo since he entered the NFL has been injuries. When he is on the field, he is highly efficient and scores plenty of fantasy points. That was on full display last season when he returned to the lineup in Week 8 off a torn ACL and went on to average 2.67 yards per route run and 11.5 fantasy points per game. The Packers’ depth chart is thinned out entering 2026, and Matthew Golden really struggled as a rookie, giving Watson relatively weak target competition for one of the better offenses in the league. There is a shaky floor with a guy like Watson due to durability concerns, but if you are looking for a high upside WR pick in the middle rounds, Watson can be nitro to your squad.
Dynasty: The Packers gave Watson a huge 4-year, $110 million contract extension in early June. They view him as a bonafide WR1 with this type of money but we have him in the low-end WR2 territory in dynasty.

Name: Zay Flowers
Outlook: Flowers is coming off a highly efficient 2025 campaign, setting a career high in targets (118), receptions (86), and yards (1,211). Over the last two seasons, Zay has averaged 2.61 and 2.35 yards per route run. Those are some elite marks. The guy is good! Unfortunately, the one bug-a-boo for Zay in fantasy land has been TDs. Through three seasons, he has topped out at just five scores. In order to make the leap into being a real difference maker, he is going to need to find the painted box more often. Perhaps he can fall into an outlier TD year, but with Lamar stealing some attempts in the red zone and King Henry still here, that seems somewhat unlikely for 2026.
Dynasty: Flowers is in his prime, productive, and tied to one of the better offenses in the league. What’s not to like?! We are in on Zay in dynasty formats, viewing him as a top-24 guy. Name: David Montgomery

Montgomery's value received a major boost when he was traded from Detroit to Houston as the team was desperately searching for an answer inside the red zone. Monty should see 18+ touches a game and return low-end RB2 value for a team that wants to win by running the ball and leaning on their defense.

Name: Malik Nabers
Outlook: When on the field and healthy, there aren’t many better than Malik Nabers. Unfortunately, the talk all offseason about Nabers is going to be injury, specifically his recovery from ACL and meniscus surgery. It is one of fantasy football’s biggest storylines to monitor throughout the summer. We are projecting him to be limited early in the season, so just understand that Nabers will come with risk in redraft leagues, and it is not out of the question that he could start the season on the PUP list. If and when he does get back to 100%, he is likely to provide a big impact to your team, but you might need to wait longer than you would prefer.

Name: Chuba Hubbard

The efficiency numbers weren’t pretty, averaging a paltry 3.8 yards per carry with only 6% of his runs gaining 10+ yards, the fourth lowest rate among qualifying RBs. He remains a value in drafts and a low-end RB2/FLEX worth filling out your roster with.

Name: Jaylen Waddle
Outlook: For the first time in his career, Jaylen Waddle is going to be wearing a different uniform as he is now in Denver after being traded before the NFL Draft. The situation is clearly much better for scoring, playing with Sean Payton and Bo Nix versus Bobby Slowik and Malik Willis. The talent remains with Waddle, so there is upside here, but be mindful that Payton historically loves to use multiple WRs, RBs, and TEs to spread things out. We would love to say Waddle will dominate targets, but the more likely scenario is that Waddle will have to have a highly efficient season to be a difference maker for fantasy.

This time next year, he will be 28 years old, so if you are not a contender and can flip Waddle on the intrigue of the Broncos offense, it might not be a bad idea.

Name: Mike Evans

Historically speaking, there is an increased risk with veterans changing teams at this stage of their career. Name: Davante Adams

Name: Brian Thomas Jr.
Outlook: What happened here?! BTJ was amazing as a rookie, and all signs pointed to a huge breakout in Year 2. Unfortunately, Thomas’ second season in the league was plagued by inconsistency, drops, injury, and poor play overall. Ultimately, he ended up being one of fantasy’s biggest busts of 2025. Entering 2026, we are now looking at this Jags’ depth chart completely differently. Travis Hunter’s usage remains unclear, Parker Washington played so well down the stretch that you would think he has earned a role, and the team traded for Jakobi Meyers last year, then immediately signed him to an extension. Something’s gotta give in a crowded WR room. Right now, we are viewing BTJ as a potential bounce-back candidate on one of the better offenses in the league. Because of last year’s headache and the crowded WR room, his ADP is depressed, so if you are looking for a volatile mid-round option who could pay off in a big way, BTJ is worth a swing in the middle rounds.
Dynasty: There aren’t many WRs who go out and do what Brian Thomas did as a rookie. Is there cause for concern after a frustrating Year 2? Sure, but this is still a former first-round NFL WR who averaged 2.56 yards per route run as a rookie. Over the last decade, most of the guys who check both of those boxes have gone on to be fantasy difference-makers. We recognize the risk, but now that his cost is down in startups and in the trade market, it might be time to see what it takes to get him on your squad.

Name: Alec Pierce
Outlook: Get that money, Mr. Pierce! After years of being a part-time role player for the Colts, Pierce showed out in 2025 with his first 1,000-yard season and cashed a huge payday with Indy. That financial commitment from the team suggests they think he can level up as a top target without Michael Pittman, but it is worth noting that he has never been a high-volume pass catcher, even going back to his college days, so it does take some projection on that front. For context, he has never had more than 52 receptions in a single season in either the pros or in college. There is some volatility with a big play threat like Pierce, so we like pairing him with more reliable high target share guys earlier in your draft.
Dynasty: It took a few years for Pierce to develop in the NFL, so the fact that he went from a bench roster clogger to a guy who is one of the highest-paid WRs in the league is kind of wild. Nonetheless, this massive contract gives him plenty of value in dynasty formats. He is an interesting player with a wide range of outcomes, so we could see this going either direction. If you are a dynasty player who likes to play the market, it is certainly possible he has already reached peak value, so we would not fault anyone for seeing what they can get in a trade for Pierce after that huge contract.

Name: Colston Loveland

His Week 17 performance against San Francisco was one that fantasy championships were made of. We were nervous with the myriad of weapons in Chicago and how Ben Johnson’s offense would come together, but Loveland was the clear first read in the offense into the NFL playoffs. DJ Moore has left town, and Johnson proved that what he did in Detroit was not a fluke, so ranking Loveland in the top four seems 100% valid. We cannot wait to see the jump Loveland can make this season.

Name: Marvin Harrison Jr.

Fantasy gamers were hoping for that classic Year 2 breakout a season ago, but it was anything but. Marv had another lackluster season, with this one defined by inconsistency and injuries that limited him to just 12 games. Through two seasons, he is averaging just 9.3 fantasy points per game. As the former fourth-overall pick, that is just not good enough. The QB room remains a giant question mark, and Trey McBride is the alpha here. Is the risk already baked into the price?
Dynasty: Based on our studies, most WRs who become difference makers do so by Year 3. If you took Marv with the first overall pick in your rookie draft two seasons ago, it is important to reset your expectations. Through two years, he averaged 1.74 and 1.68 yards per route run. Those are solid efficiency marks, but they are middling for a former top-five overall pick. If Marv can’t break out this year, he is probably settling into WR3ville for fantasy.

Name: Jadarian Price
Outlook: After much speculation before the NFL Draft, the Seahawks took the Notre Dame RB with the final pick of Round 1. Price’s selection looks even stronger, knowing we did not see another RB drafted until the end of Round 3. His initial burst plus his contact balance make for a lethal combo, although he was not utilized much in the passing game in college, with just 15 total receptions over three years. With Zach Charbonnet on the mend from an ACL injury, Price found arguably the best landing spot for a rookie RB in 2026. The Seahawks are favored in each of their first 10 matchups. We have him ranked as a high-end FLEX option with the expectation that his draft stock could rise throughout the summer.
Dynasty: As Winston Churchill once said, some things are "a riddle, wrapped in a mystery, inside an enigma.” Price was a confusing prospect to evaluate as the backup to Jeremiyah Love with minimal college production, and yet became only the second RB over the last decade drafted in Rounds 1-3 that had fewer than 700 rushing yards in his final college season. Opportunity is everything, and on a Super Bowl-winning team, he should have the chance to be this team’s lead back of the future.

Name: Jaylen Warren

While he ceded passing downs work to Kenneth Gainwell down the stretch, it was the best fantasy season of Warren’s four-year career, thanks to an increase in workload. He was rather pedestrian inside the 5-yard line, converting only four of his 14 carries. His outlook for 2026 is souring with the signing of Rico Dowdle and the arrival of a new coaching regime. He is stuck in a complicated backfield mess, profiling more as a FLEX, knowing he could take a backseat to Dowdle at some point.
Dynasty: The former UDFA is under contract for two more seasons, although his dead cap hit in 2027 ($1.9 million) gives the team an out. He feels like an RB you are just happy has held FLEX value for a few seasons. Name: Parker Washington

Following the bye week and Travis Hunter’s injury, Washington saw a 23% target share while running roughly half his routes from the slot. We are really interested in taking shots on this Jaguars’ offense, but it is nearly impossible to project the WR usage here, especially with Travis Hunter’s role up in the air. As a result of that uncertainty, expect the Jaguars guys to all be decent values in drafts. There is some downside with each individual WR here, but if you hit on the right guy, the payoff could be massive, especially at these depressed ADPs.

He did see 58 targets (tied for the fifth most among Day 2 RBs over the last decade), giving you hope that his value will hold in full PPR leagues. Nevertheless, he likely saw his value peak already in dynasty.

Name: Chris Godwin Jr.
Outlook: Can we go into Madden and turn off injuries here? Before that nasty ankle fracture-dislocation in 2024, Godwin was putting up some unreal numbers, but ever since that injury, his efficiency has taken a hit, with his yards per reception declining in three straight seasons. Mike Evans’ departure theoretically opens up a world where Godwin can bounce back, so we do not mind adding him in drafts as a WR3/4 for your squad, but the upside we have seen in the past is unlikely to repeat at this stage of his career. He certainly gets a small boost in full PPR leagues.
Dynasty: Godwin has been a dynasty stalwart for years, but those days are long gone, mostly because of injury and age. Rod God is now 30 years old, and he is coming off back-to-back injury-affected seasons while averaging just 3.7 catches per game. Sadly, we view him as an expiring asset, even if he is able to have a solid 2026 campaign.

Name: Jordan Mason
Outlook: Mason continues to show he belongs in the NFL, averaging 5.1 yards per carry since coming into the league in 2022. For context, only De’Von Achane (5.6) and Jahmyr Gibbs (5.3) are higher in that span. After averaging 16 opportunities per game through the first six games, he eventually fell behind Aaron Jones Sr. in the RB rotation down the stretch in a lost season for the Vikings. Mason is older than you might realize (27 years old), but is only an injury away from being an every-down workhorse.
Dynasty: Mason is a fun dynasty story to share as savvy managers read the tea leaves of the last few years and hopefully recouped value as a startable RB2/FLEX when needed. Signed in free agency last year, Mason has one more year on his deal before the void years kick in. He carries a number of rushing yardage incentives (800/900/1,000 yards) in his contract that could come into play down the stretch.

Name: Xavier Worthy
Outlook: Worthy had a chance in 2025 to really take a step forward to start the season while Rashee Rice was serving his suspension. Unfortunately, in the first quarter of the first game of the season, Worthy dislocated his shoulder and never looked right, playing through injury all season. You can certainly explain away some of the down production last year, but truth be told, we still do not know how good a player he is in the league. In two seasons, he is averaging 1.40 yards per route run and earning targets on 18% of his routes. As the kids say, those numbers are pretty mid. Still, we are always interested in taking shots on Mahomes’ pass catchers, and the Chiefs didn’t add any meaningful talent to the WR room this offseason. Entering the season, he is a boom-bust FLEX option.
Dynasty: Worthy had promise as a first-round rookie WR, but through two seasons in the league, his metrics suggest he is unlikely to turn into a fantasy difference maker. That said, Travis Kelce is another year older, and Rashee Rice is in a contract year. If you were a Worthy truther coming out of Texas, he is probably worth a look in the trade market, but it is a make-or-break season for Worthy’s future dynasty prospects.

Name: Tucker Kraft
Outlook: Kraft was on his way to a monster season in 2025, boasting two TE1 weeks and averaging close to six targets a game, until he tore his ACL in Week 9 against Carolina. He was Green Bay’s leading receiver with 32 catches for 489 yards and six TDs on 44 targets up until that point. Despite his major injury, you will still have to pay up to draft Kraft, making him a dicey draft decision. The talent is there, and the targets could be even easier to come by, but also factor in that Kraft might not be ready to go by Week 1.
Dynasty: Of course, Kraft is ahead of schedule and reported to be back early in the regular season, targeting a Week 1 return. Shocking. 2026 marks the final year of his deal with the Packers, but GM Brian Gutekunst has made it clear that talks regarding a contract extension are underway. Both Romeo Doubs and Dontayvion Wicks have moved on, bumping Kraft up in the target pecking order, and the Green Bay offense should continue to produce at a high level with Jordan Love at the helm. Kraft is a YAC king and a great option if you want to wait in a startup past the big TE names.

Name: Kyle Pitts Sr.

Week 15 blowout game against Tampa Bay. We are not expecting this from Pitts heading into 2026, so make sure to let your league mates reach for him come draft day. Rankings-wise, he is living in that TE6-TE10 range where players can become almost interchangeable. Aside from Bijan Robinson and Drake London, the Falcons do not have much target competition, so a world exists where he could bolster his consistency and build on his 2025 season. The QB situation and a new offensive play-caller bring more risk and uncertainty than we would like.

That move (among others) cost this Atlanta front office their jobs and likely sunk your dynasty team for years. The man turns 26 this year, and many of us are hoping he heads to a different team after this season with an opportunity for a killer second act.

Name: J.K. Dobbins
Outlook: Poor J.K… can this guy catch a break? Through the first ten weeks of the season, he was the RB18, averaging 16.7 opportunities per game ahead of the more hyped RJ Harvey. An unfortunate “hip-drop” tackle ended his season yet again, as he now has appeared in just 38% of possible games over the last five years. He signed a two-year deal worth up to $20 million to run it back with the Broncos. While we still expect Harvey to see high-value looks in the pass game, Dobbins should have FLEX-worthy weeks in the right matchup while running behind one of the best offensive lines in football.

White was the guy a couple of seasons ago, but he was relegated to a change-of-pace option with the emergence of the more efficient Bucky Irving and Sean Tucker over the last two years. We know his place as a pass-catcher, and he might be going overlooked in drafts despite our projections having him as a FLEX option.

That investment says he will remain a key piece of the team moving forward, but his snap share could be frustrating in 2026 with Cooper Kupp still on the roster. Name: Travis Kelce
Outlook: Quietly the TE4 in 2025, Kelce finished as the TE8 or better every year as a starter, reminding us he is a true fantasy football legend. Louder for the people in the back! He had a solid 851 yards receiving (TE4) with 584 routes run (TE2), and you can continue to expect to see him on the field as Patrick Mahomes’ good ole security blanket. We would like to see that TD total go up a bit - Kelce had five last year - but to be fair, no one on the team caught more than that number. He is a solid later-round pick if you wait on the TE position, knowing this is his latest ADP in more than a decade. Respect your elders.

That won’t necessarily equate to high-end fantasy success as you ride off into the sunset with the all-timer.

Of course, most of us were not expecting much from Gainwell given his track record and the minimal investment, but goodness gracious, he was a force in PPR formats thanks to his team-leading 73 receptions. The only backs with more catches? CMC, Bijan, and Jahmyr Gibbs. He is a valuable commodity as a FLEX option, especially if Bucky Irving fails to regain his rookie-year form.

Name: Blake Corum

However, in his sophomore campaign, he siphoned off 159 total opportunities for a 35% share of the carries. Kyren is obviously the lead back in this offense, but it is entirely possible Corum pushes that number closer to the 40 - 45% mark. If he does, he could have standalone value as a weekly FLEX play. Of course, should Kyren go down, Corum would project as a no-brainer weekly RB1, so he is also one of fantasy’s most valuable insurance policies while playing for one of the best offenses in the league.

Name: Aaron Jones Sr.

He remains under contract for one more year in Minnesota, sharing the backfield with Jordan Mason. The Vikings begin the season with a rough three-week stretch (GB, @ CHI, @ TB) before things open up. We have him ranked as a low-end FLEX option with unknowns at the QB and betting that the wheels finally fall off of his insanely efficient career on a per-touch basis.
Dynasty: Jones is hitting that RB age cliff (31 years old) and only has one more year on his deal. You likely will get nothing of value in a trade, so we recommend holding in hopes he bounces back in the TD department and the Minnesota offense returns to its norm with Kyler Murray under center.

He certainly is capable of being a fantasy-friendly asset thanks to his pass-catching abilities, but we project him to firmly be the RB2 behind Skattebo. Name: George Kittle

If anyone can do it, you know it is Kittle; he has given us six seasons where he has finished as the TE5 or higher! The physical freak who has publicly said he is targeting an early-season return could be a value in fantasy drafts, if he drops far enough. Pair him with a vanilla-reliable TE that you can use confidently in the first half of your season, knowing you can pop Kittle on your IR. If he comes back even close to his past form late in the season, you have some options in your playoff push. We don’t suggest even entertaining the option unless you have multiple IR spots.
Dynasty: If you have Kittle in dynasty, you are holding as you would only be getting pennies on the dollar if you trade him. You might get lucky to obtain another solid year from him in 2027 post-Achilles, and after that, it might just be a “thank you so much for your service” farewell.

He now finds himself on a scary trajectory entering Year 2. Historically, WRs with poor efficiency in their rookie season who post virtually zero production have flopped more often than not in the NFL. That doesn’t mean it cannot work for Golden; probability is just not on his side. That said, the Packers are absolutely counting on him after they traded Dontayvion Wicks to the Eagles and let Romeo Doubs walk in free agency. Consider Golden a complete wild card entering 2026.

He had plenty of red flags in his production profile coming out of college, and then in Year 1, he averaged just 1.35 yards per route run as a part-time player. Entering his second season, odds are stacked against Golden, but the opportunity is certainly there, and the Packers invested a first-round pick in him just a year ago. If he flops again in Year 2, it is probably not going to happen, but the market is down on him, so he is an interesting buy low with the risk already baked into his startup ADP.

We know Garrett Wilson and Breece Hall are likely to dominate touches here, so any secondary Jets players are probably going to have to get there via efficiency. Call us crazy, but if your eyeballs saw Geno playing QB last season, we are a little hesitant with that bet! That is where the concern comes in for a guy like Cooper, who profiles as a possession and yards after the catch WR in the NFL. So long as Garrett Wilson is healthy, Cooper’s fantasy upside will be capped, but you could always target him late in drafts and see what shakes out in New York early in the season. Rookie WRs are generally good bets at ADP because the cost is reasonable, but if we are comparing Cooper to the other first-round NFL draft picks, he is not as big of a priority as guys like Carnell Tate, Makai Lemon, or Jordyn Tyson.
Dynasty: Cooper was a bit of a late bloomer at Indiana, but he was a big riser throughout the pre-draft process after Indiana’s National Championship run. He ultimately went in the back of Round 1 of the NFL Draft, which helps his stock obviously, but this is a player who didn’t clear 30 catches in a single season until his fourth year in college. There are some things to like about Cooper, but he does come with some risk in dynasty circles.

There is room for those market share numbers to grow in Year 2, but it is worth noting that Tank Dell might return to the lineup, Xavier Hutchinson is also part of this team, and Jaylin Noel could also see some more snaps this season as well. Stroud bounces back to support multiple fantasy-relevant WRs.

His Year 1 peripherals aren’t amazing, but they aren’t terrible either. In the three games where he posted an 80+ percent route share, he scored double-digit PPR points in all three of those games, and on the season, he averaged a respectable 1.45 yards per route run. Those numbers leave the light on that Higgins can develop as a pro, but obviously, there is a wide range of outcomes here moving forward.

Dynasty: Njoku turns 30 this year and is coming off a knee injury that robbed him of the final four games of the 2025 season. If someone is offering up something for him trade-wise after he has a decent game, it might be time to send Njoku away with a thank-you note and a box of chocolates.

Name: Mike Gesicki
Outlook: Remember those magical “Sticky Gesicki” weeks a few seasons back? Never forget. Gesicki’s great weeks in 2024 primarily made their appearances when Tee Higgins was out of the lineup, and then in 2025, things got even more confusing with QB Joe Burrow missing nine weeks. Gesicki only had a 9.4% target share last season and will always be left battling on that front with talents like Ja’Marr Chase and Higgins on the field. If he is on your roster, you are only entertaining starting him if one of those WRs is out, and for a guy who only had two receiving TDs in 2025, you don’t need that nonsense.
Dynasty: There is so much that has to go a certain way for Gesicki to be relevant in dynasty. Sure, he might be the guy to start on the third Sunday of October when the moon is in its waning phase, and the wind is blowing at exactly 2.2 mph, but c’mon, who has the time for that? The team has more youth on their side with Erick All Jr. (25) and rookie Jack Endries (22), making it even easier to leave Gesicki on your bench.

Even though Mike Evans is gone, Egbuka and Godwin are still here, and Jalen McMillan has been productive whenever he has been healthy. The opportunity opens up in 2027, but we are not expecting much production in Year 1. He is a solid end-of-bench stash.

Name: Jalen McMillan

Miami desperately needs pass-catchers considering a WR core of Malik Washington, Jalen Tolbert, and Tutu Atwell in South Florida. Name: Eli Stowers

After a final year at Vandy, where he won the 2025 John Mackey award as college football’s top TE, Stowers' production profile was quite strong, leading the class in targets per route run. Stowers will be listed as a TE, but he is essentially a big hybrid WR/TE who actually began college as a QB. He will be behind Dallas Goedert on the depth chart, making him hard to roster in redraft in 2026 outside of an injury.

Saquon’s sun may also be setting soon as the team's aged identity looks set to transition. Stowers will be a big part of their future, and if you were lucky enough to snag him in dynasty, he could be the answer to your TE position for a significant amount of time. We have him ranked as a top-12 dynasty TE asset to start 2026.

Name: Kimani Vidal
Outlook: Vidal Sassoon! To this man’s credit, he went from being inactive in the first few games behind Omarion Hampton and Najee Harris to becoming one of the more valuable adds off the waiver wire in 2025. After Harris went down for the season and Hampton suffered a serious ankle injury, Vidal stepped in as the lead back and finished as a top-24 fantasy RB in five of eight games where he played 60+ percent of the snaps. It is nice to know that if the opportunity arises, he is fully capable of handling 15+ touches as a plug-and-play option. Of course, we are not projecting much workload with Omarion Hampton atop the depth chart and Keaton Mitchell joining the squad in free agency, so he is not worth a draft pick, but if Hampton happens to go down, he will be a priority add off the waiver wire.
Dynasty: Vidal is back in LA on a one-year deal as a restricted free agent, so it remains to be seen where he will play in 2027. Regardless, he is a fine player to put at the end of your bench, but you are obviously not counting on him for any production or long-term value.
