Name: Kyle Williams
Outlook: The 3rd round pick was barely involved as a rookie and now with the arrival of A.J. Brown and Romeo Doubs, he looks very unlikely to be a fantasy asset in 2026. Sorry Williams truthers but a 4% target share as a rookie is not going to get it done.
Dynasty: We kind of liked Williams as a fun prospect last year, but as things go in dynasty land, there are way more misses than hits. Unless Williams does a 180 in Year 2, it is bleak, folks. He caught just 10 passes as a rookie despite appearing in all 17 games. Ew.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Pat Bryant
Outlook: Bryant did very little early in the season, but as the year went on, he started to pop as the team scaled back Troy Franklin’s role. Unfortunately, a concussion and hamstring injury limited his production late in the year, so entering Year 2, there are still question marks on Bryant as a player. He will have a role, but it is highly unlikely to be fantasy relevant now that Jaylen Waddle is on this roster. Consider him a potential waiver wire pickup if Waddle and/or Sutton miss time.
Dynasty: Bryant’s dynasty value took a huge hit when the Broncos sent a first round pick for Jaylen Waddle. He’s a fun prospect who flashed at times as a rookie, so we would like to stash him if possible; It just might take a year or two for him to get a full-time role after Courtland Sutton ages out.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Mack Hollins
Outlook: If your league awards points for aura, Mack Daddy is the 1.01. The shoeless wonder is a weekly must-see for his interesting stadium entrances. He was a solid player for New England on their Super Bowl run a year ago, but with AJ Brown joining the squad, he is not on the radar for fantasy.
Dynasty: We are huge fans of Hollins’ Instagram account, where he shares all sorts of useful info for everyday tasks labeled #MackHacks. Oh, you came here for his dynasty value? It does not exist. But seriously, give the guy a follow on IG, and thank us later.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Dawson Knox
Outlook: It is super cool being tied to Josh Allen and a high-flying offense, but neither is enough to push Knox into the draftable range in redraft. It has been a good long while since his TE8 season of 2021. Never forget.
Dynasty: You can’t drop that guy because you know what happens if one or two guys go down to injury ahead of him. Find him a cozy corner on your bench, knowing Knox is under contract for two more years in Buffalo as Josh Allen’s best bud.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Mason Taylor
Outlook: Meow. Would you be shocked to find out that Mason Taylor led the Jets in targets in 2025? Me. Too. The main reason was that Garrett Wilson was shut down early due to injury, and there was truly nothing else in town offensively. The second round selection in 2025 is immediately trounced by the drafting of athletic freak TE Kenyon Sadiq. Find Taylor a patch of sunshine and let him curl up. He does not belong in fantasy.
Dynasty: The man with zero broken tackles might have a short tenure in New York as the organization seemingly drafted his replacement, Sadiq, with the 16th overall pick in 2026. You don’t need to hold onto this kitty in dynasty when a dawg is in town.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jonathon Brooks
Outlook: Brooks enters his age-23 season with 12 total touches over two seasons since he was taken 46th overall. We have not seen him on the football field since Week 14 of the 2024 season after he brutally re-injured his ACL. Monitor training camp news and beat reporters to see how involved he could be in 2026. Rico Dowdle vacates 51% of the carries and 10% of the team’s targets, giving credence to Brooks being a sleeper name to stash at the end of your drafts. We always loved the talent, but for now, we still have projected behind Chuba Hubbard in Carolina.
Dynasty: He is one of the great mysteries of dynasty after he was the first RB taken in the 2024 NFL Draft and then vanished from relevance. His value is also a complete wildcard as he ranges from potentially overtaking Chuba to fading back into oblivion. If you have Brooks, he is a hold in hopes he resurrects from his fantasy grave.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Ty Johnson
Outlook: Johnson will always mean more to the Buffalo Bills than your fantasy teams. He ranked fifth among all RBs in the NFL in routes run on third down and did see an annoyingly high 23 opportunities inside the red zone. His biggest contribution to fantasy will be watching the TV and screaming that your RB target went to “Johnson” instead of “Cook”. Even in the deepest of PPR leagues, Ty is just a guy.
Dynasty: The Bills must love this guy, as they continually deploy in their game plans to poke fun at fantasy managers. Johnson is under contract for one more year and figures to see a quarter of the RB snaps.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Michael Mayer
Outlook: Always the bridesmaid, never the bride. Mayer will be the TE2 as long as Brock Bowers is a Raider. But wait, you say…Mayer must have tons of value when Bowers is out of the lineup, right? RIGHT? He did give you one TE5 week when Bowers was sidelined last year, but during the other week, he was TE41. This former 2nd round NFL draft pick will be valuable in 12-personnel sets to the Raiders but not for fantasy.
Dynasty: The majority of Mayer’s dynasty value went poof the moment that Bowers was drafted, and he has done nothing to build it back up. The blocking TE in Las Vegas need not be on your fantasy roster.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Nicholas Singleton
Outlook: The former #1 RB recruit in the nation finished his four-year career at Penn State as Penn State's all-time rushing TD leader with 45! Unfortunately, after suffering a foot injury during a Senior Bowl practice, Singleton's draft stock fell to the fifth round. If you believe in his size/speed combo, perhaps he can eventually overtake Tony Pollard and Tyjae Spears and turn into a team's lead back in the back half of the season.
Dynasty: We continually point out the fact that the stickiest stat from college to the pros is TD production, so Singleton certainly checks that box. When you add in over 100 career receptions, there is a lot to get excited about. The question is whether these stats simply make him a compiler or point to a difference-maker at the next level? The draft capital might not sound appealing (fifth round), but the context of the landing spot is what is most appealing. Pollard and Spears will be free agents in 2027, making him a worthy dice roll in dynasty.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: James Conner
Outlook: It has been a wonderful run for Conner, but it seems he finally will fade into the fantasy background. After four straight years as a top-20 RB and the engine of the Arizona offense, the 31-year-old missed the majority of 2025 with a right foot injury, and it seems he will be buried on the depth chart behind Tyler Allgeier and rookie Jeremiyah Love.
Dynasty: Conner is under contract for one more year. Rather than moving on from the 31-year-old and saving over $7 million against the cap, the team decided to restructure his deal to keep him on the roster for 2026.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jahan Dotson
Outlook: Super Bowl champion and Eagles legend, Jahan Dotson, is now in Atlanta after signing a two-year deal in free agency. Behind Drake London, this WR room is full of some #NastyBoys, and Dotson might be the nastiest of them all. THE cardio king, Dotson has averaged an embarrasingly low 0.63 yards per route run over the last two seasons. It’s hard to put into words how bad that is…
Dynasty: If you are still holding Jahan Dotson in your dynasty league, it is time. Set him free and never look back!
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Chimere Dike
Outlook: Dike provided a ton of value for the Titans, especially in the return game. In fact, the guy set the NFL rookie record for the most all-purpose yards in a single season with 2,427 scrimmage yards in 2025. He is electric in the return game, and that is probably where he will shine as Carnell Tate, Wan’Dale Robinson, and Calvin Ridley run the most routes. He is not fantasy relevant right now, but if one of the WRs goes down to injury, we at least know he can play.
Dynasty: Dike is a dart throw/end-of-bench stash in dynasty formats. Obviously, his outlook is capped with Carnell Tate going fourth overall and Wan’Dale signing that big contract in free agency. Perhaps he can carve out the WR3 role over time.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Braelon Allen
Outlook: Honest question: Do you have any memory of Allen contributing in the 2025 season? If you do, you are either a “Rain Man” or a diabolical liar, as he touched the ball 20 total times before bowing out for the rest of the season to an MCL sprain. He is a man-child and an impressive physical specimen, but likely nothing more than a dart throw in your drafts.
Dynasty: At just 22 years old, Allen remains one of the youngest RBs in the league despite having “two years” of NFL experience. You can start connecting the dots with a tin foil hat on and see a path for Allen to be a fantasy monster if Breece Hall went down.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Luke Musgrave
Outlook: The Packers double-dipped in the TE in the 2023 NFL Draft, and even though Tucker Kraft has had the breakout season, it was Musgrave whom the team took earlier (second round). Musgrave had a chance to show why he was drafted earlier when Kraft went down with his ACL injury in Week 9, but he did nothing with the opportunity. He averaged just over two targets a game from Week 10 on, only marginally better than the 1.25 a week he was averaging with Kraft on the field. Either way you slice it, not great.
Dynasty: Musgrave’s dynasty window is only slightly propped open as he enters the final year of his rookie deal. Unable to capitalize on Kraft’s missing time in 2025, it is hard to believe that he can do it while sharing time with him in 2026.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Theo Johnson
Outlook: Johnson was putting together a decent sophomore season in 2025, highlighted by an average of 4.9 targets a game and a budding connection with Jaxson Dart. However, he missed the final two weeks of the season with an unspecified illness, ending the year with a whimper. All was looking promising until John Harbaugh came to New York and brought Isaiah Likely with him. Now Johnson is the TE2, dropping out of the redraft conversation. What a roller coaster.
Dynasty: It is frustrating with Johnson, as it feels like the promise of a future TE1 spot on his team was pulled out from under him with the signing of Likely. He is still young and with the Giants on his rookie deal through 2027. We can hope that Harbaugh will utilize more two-TE sets in New York, as he did in Baltimore. Consider Johnson a hold in dynasty, but his window is likely gone. (See what we did there :)
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Zachariah Branch
Outlook: We know Drake London is the dude here in Atlanta, but behind him, this depth chart is wide open. The Falcons used the 79th overall pick on the former Georgia Bulldog, so he has a realistic opportunity to see the field as a rookie. Electric in the return game and dangerous with the ball in his hands, Branch was used primarily in the screen game and on manufactured touches in college. He is going to have to develop in the NFL to be a factor for fantasy, but perhaps he can be a late-season riser.
Dynasty: If Branch is going to work for fantasy in the long run, he is going to have to be an outlier. He is an undersized speed WR, and 45% of his career targets in college came behind the line of scrimmage, with over 35% of his career yards coming via screens. Yikes! This is the exact type of profile that has burned dynasty players in the past, but when you look at the state of this depth chart behind Drake London, you can squint and see a path. We consider him a dart-throw type of stash in dynasty formats.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Terrance Ferguson
Outlook: Ferg-Daddy 2.0 is one of many TEs in Sean McVay’s crowded TE room, including the likes of Tyler Higbee, Colby Parkinson, and rookie Max Klare. We had high hopes for him in his rookie season. He didn’t deliver, running the fourth fewest routes among the team’s TEs and gaining only a 5% target share.  Ferguson did show off via the deep ball as he was the league leader among TEs in deep targets (20+ yards) with 15. Tyler Higbee and Davante Adams are not getting any younger, so perhaps we see a patented second-year breakout from the Oregon TE.
Dynasty: A young TE tied to a prolific offense run by a guy who loves to use multiple TEs? Ferguson sounds like someone worth holding on to, at least for a couple of years, to see what he turns into. He has not played his way into weekly starter status, but hey, Colby Parkinson has definitely given us some startable weeks. Just saying.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Xavier Legette
Outlook: Legette lost his WR2 job last year to Jalen Coker, and we are projecting that to stick in 2026. Tetairoa McMillan should dominate the target share here, and Coker is a fine WR2 option for the team. Call us crazy, but we are not drafting Bryce Young’s WR3, especially after he saw four or fewer targets in eight of his final 10 games to close 2025.
Dynasty: This man’s accent is fun, and he is a good time. That is about where the positives end for Legette’s dynasty outlook. Through two seasons, he is averaging 1.11 YPRR. I think we all know where this is headed…

Name: Sean Tucker
Outlook: Tucker had a few shining moments in 2025, including a monster RB1 overall performance in Buffalo in Week 11. He can flash at times and also led the team in carries (14) and rushing TDs (6) inside the 10-yard line. However, the Buccaneers backfield needs to move from a three-headed monster (Bucky, Kenny G & the Tuckmeister) to two in order for him to be started with any confidence. Expect Tucker to be floating on waiver wires after your drafts.
Dynasty: Tucker signed a one-year deal worth $3.52 million to return as the team’s preferred goal-line back. There is a place for that on a dynasty bench, as he is better suited for standard or half-point formats.

Name: Ja'Tavion Sanders
Outlook: Sanders is recovering from a broken fibula and will be battling with Tommy Tremble for the top option at the TE position in Carolina this season. You don’t really want a TE on a team with a QB who averaged under 200 yards passing a week, do you? Do you?
Dynasty: Inconsistent target volume makes you nervous holding onto Sanders in dynasty. The team could move on from Tremble after 2026, so you could tentatively hold onto Sanders to see how the depth chart plays out. You would really need Bryce Young to take a step forward to give you any sense of confidence in Sanders.

Name: DJ Giddens
Outlook: The former fifth-round pick is the presumed backup to Jonathan Taylor in Indy. However, we confess that his rookie season does not give us a ton of information to work from, as he touched the ball 26 total times and was active in three total games from Week 8 on. He is an explosive athlete with the opportunity to lock up the RB2 spot. For now, know the name, but the JTT manager should not feel obligated to draft him even as insurance.
Dynasty: The size (6’0”, 212 lbs.) and speed combo (4.43 40-time) made Giddens an intriguing dynasty name a year ago. If Taylor were to go down, he could vault into being an every-down guy, but that remains mere speculation. In the words of Cosmo Kramer, “Giddy Up,” DJ!
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Kaelon Black
Outlook: Despite a lackluster production profile, Black was a hot commodity in pre-draft visits, meeting with 10 teams before the 49ers surprisingly selected him 90th overall. Kyle Shanahan offenses always surprise us with the names that leap from obscurity, but this one was a head scratcher. CMC is coming off a season where he touched the ball 413 times, so perhaps we see Black worked into the mix. Pay attention to camp reports to see where he lands on the depth chart, competing with Jordan James and Isaac Guerendo for backup duties.
Dynasty: The Indiana RB was the lesser-known of their National Championship-winning backfield duo, and yet Roman Hemby went undrafted. We have a history of failed 49ers RB draft picks, but Black should be evaluated based on his profile, not their lack of success. He belongs in the mid-second round of drafts, but RB-thirsty managers might push him higher.

Name: Dontayvion Wicks
Outlook: The Eagles traded for Dontayvion Wicks and immediately signed him to a one-year extension as he has some familiarity with new OC Sean Mannion, who comes over from Green Bay. We ultimately expect him to be mixing in with Hollywood Brown on the perimeter, but unless Jalen Hurts all of a sudden becomes a prolific passer, Wicks isn’t likely to be a meaningful fantasy contributor behind Smitty and Makai Lemon.
Dynasty: Wicks has always flashed target-earning potential (and drops), and he is just 24 years old. You could probably throw him in your starting lineup should DeVonta Smith or Makai Lemon miss some time, so if you are in a deeper league, you could certainly keep him on the end of your bench. Philly signed him to a one-year extension worth $12.5 million after trading for him earlier this spring.

Name: Jaylin Noel
Outlook: Houston double-dipped in the 2025 NFL Draft on Day 2, taking both Iowa State WR prospects. They grabbed Jayden Higgins in Round 2 and Noel in Round 3, and neither guy really stepped up for fantasy on a consistent basis. Noel was stuck behind Christian Kirk for the slot role last year, but with Kirk gone, he has a chance to win that slot role.
Dynasty: Noel is a fine stash right now until we see if he can make a jump in Year 2. It is a small sample size, but the efficiency marks aren’t terrible (1.50 YPRR, 8.3 YPT) for a former Day 2 pick.

Name: Marvin Mims Jr.
Outlook: This guy is so fun to watch when he is making an explosive play, but through three years, he has played just 32% of the snaps. Darn you, Sean Payton! You tricked us thrice into thinking he could actually be something! We know now, he is pretty much just their kick returner.
Dynasty: Mims will always be remembered in dynasty formats on the promise of “Sean Payton traded up in the second round to draft this guy.” Sadly, he’s just a part-time player who will never have any real value. Oh, what could have been!

Name: Tez Johnson
Outlook: Tiny Tez! This guy flashed when his number was called in 2025, as Mike Evans and Chris Godwin missed a bunch of time last season. Unfortunately, unless you are an outlier, former seventh-round picks who weigh 165 pounds don’t usually go on to be fantasy stars. Tampa probably views him as a depth piece behind Egbuka, Godwin, McMillan, and Ted Hurst.
Dynasty: Tez Johnson actually had a really solid production profile coming out of Oregon, and you saw in his rookie year that he belongs in the league. That doesn’t necessarily mean he is worth a roster spot in dynasty, as he is buried on the depth chart.

Name: Ja'Kobi Lane
Outlook: Lane saw a bunch of contested catches at USC, which is interesting considering his slender build. Expect him to start the season behind Flowers and Rashod Bateman in a part-time role with the chance to develop over the course of his rookie season. For now, he is not worth looking at in fantasy drafts.
Dynasty: The production profile for Lane is pretty underwhelming, but he is an early declare prospect who played next to Makai Lemon at USC, so we will leave the light on for him in dynasty formats as a stash. This is a new coaching staff, and Rashod Bateman hasn’t exactly been a prolific weapon for the team in recent years. If he surprises as a rookie, there is a path, but it’s a pretty thin bet.

Name: Malachi Fields
Outlook: The Giants traded multiple picks to land Notre Dame’s Malachi Fields, so the new staff likely has a role in mind for him. At 6’5” and 218 pounds, he is a true X receiver. Unfortunately, for fantasy, Fields looks like a guy who is more likely to help the Giants than help our fantasy teams, as he is a pretty weak prospect coming out of Notre Dame. We know Malik Nabers is the alpha when healthy, and the team paid big money to Isaiah Likely in free agency. We would be surprised if he is able to generate enough volume to matter for fantasy, but he could have some TD upside in the right matchups thanks to his power forward frame.
Dynasty: Analytically, Fields doesn’t have a lot going for him in terms of numbers. He is an older prospect who specializes as a field stretcher and contested catch target. Generally speaking, those profiles haven’t really hit for dynasty, but with Wan’Dale Robinson gone and Darius Slayton likely not around for much longer, the landing spot says we need to keep the light on over the next 1-2 years, as he could be the second option across from Malik Nabers.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Ray Davis
Outlook: After a steady rookie season backing up James Cook, Davis saw his usage curiously cut in half last year, averaging just 15% of the snaps and just four touches per game despite dressing for all 17 regular-season games. He is nothing more than an insurance policy for Cook managers as he fights for playing time alongside third-down back Ty Johnson.
Dynasty: Entering Year 3, Davis has a tough path to carving out any fantasy relevance. His early down skillset is directly blocked by Cook and Buffalo’s true goal-line back, Josh Allen. If Cook were to miss time, we would assume Davis would be the heir apparent to being the lead back in this Buffalo rushing attack.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Brashard Smith
Outlook: The seventh-round pick from 2025 was a trendy sleeper pick last year, as fantasy managers cited “well, Isaiah Pacheco was a seventh-round pick too!” Smith was sparingly used all season, touching the ball 79 total times despite the uninspiring play of veterans Kareem Hunt and Pacheco. With Kenneth Walker III now aboard, he is back to being a rotational back with little path forward in fantasy.
Dynasty: Welp, there goes his window in dynasty. The former WR turned RB tantalized with his production profile and speed (4.39 40-time), but is barely holding value as a bench player. Do not feel the need to roster him apart from the deepest of PPR leagues.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Elijah Sarratt
Outlook: There are talks of Sarratt moving into the ‘Big Slot’ role in Year 1 in Baltimore, which is kind of intriguing in deep formats. Sarratt is a ball winner who was frequently used in the red zone in college, giving him life as a contributor right away as a rookie in Baltimore. That said, we expect Flowers and Bateman to open the season as the top WRs on the depth chart, and the Ravens have never had a high pass rate in the Lamar Jackson era, so the path for fantasy is pretty thin out of the gate. He is not on the radar right now as a redraft target, but know the name for the waiver wire in the event Flowers or Bateman miss time due to injury.
Dynasty: Baltimore double-dipped in the NFL Draft, taking Ja’Kobi Lane in Round 3, then Sarratt in Round 4. Even though the Ravens took Lane first, you could argue Sarratt is the better prospect for the game we play. Not only did he catch 15 TDs for the National Champion Hoosiers, but he also had a 2.56 career yards per route run rate and was second in the entire class behind only Makai Lemon in first downs per route run.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Xavier Hutchinson
Outlook: We all assumed last year’s rookies, Jayden Higgins and Jaylin Noel, would steal playing time from boring vets like Xavier Hutchinson, but he was able to keep his role all season, playing 58% of the snaps. Unfortunately, he is not an efficient player and has averaged less than 13 receiving yards per game in his career (Hey, don’t laugh). He could mix in for snaps again this season, but that does not mean he will be fantasy viable.
Dynasty: Hutchinson is entering the final year of his rookie deal with Houston, so he will likely be looking for work on the open market next season as the Texans look to Jayden Higgins and Jaylin Noel (and maybe Tank Dell?) as their future depth pieces behind Nico Collins.

Name: Jaylen Wright
Outlook: After two seasons backing up De’Von Achane in Miami, it is clear Wright is not cut from the same cloth. He was drafted for his speed (4.38 40-time), but is averaging only 3.9 yards per carry and caught eight total passes. He is a backup RB on a team currently favored in just one of their 17 games. Translation: he is not on our redraft radar.
Dynasty: With Mike McDaniel out the door, any semblance of hope you had for the Dolphins carrying a 1-2 punch at RB is gonezo. The curse of players coming from the University of Tennessee's college offensive system continues to plague teams that drafted them. He is rosterable but will not be usable apart from an Achane injury.

Name: De'Zhaun Stribling
Outlook: The Niners shocked everyone by taking De’Zhaun Stribling 33rd overall in the NFL Draft. He was projected by most as a Day 2 guy, but no one saw this coming. It suggests they have a plan for him, but as long as Mike Evans and Ricky Pearsall are healthy (which is no sure thing), Stribling figures to be more of a role player in Year 1.
Dynasty: From a dynasty outlook, the draft capital here is doing A LOT of the heavy lifting. Analytically, Stribling is a pretty weak prospect. He is already 23 years old after playing five collegiate seasons, and in those five years, he cleared 850 receiving yards just once. One area where he does excel is after the catch, which is usually what the 49ers look for in their WRs. He is also good in the blocking game…sure sounds like a Kyle Shanahan receiver, doesn’t it? All in all, we are not expecting much in Year 1, and analytically, it is an uphill battle for this to work. That said, if ever there was a scheme to get the most out of Stribling, it’s this one in San Fran.

Name: Emanuel Wilson
Outlook: Wilson was quietly better than Josh Jacobs on a number of efficiency metrics. He is coming off a career high 142 total opportunities in Green Bay and flashed in a major way with his lone start in Week 12 with Jacobs sidelined. His addition to the Seattle backfield is noteworthy given Kenneth Walker’s departure and Zach Charbonnet’s ACL tear. There is a massive opportunity up for grabs in this Seahawks backfield, although first-round pick Jadarian Price should eventually be the lead back. Wilson could gain more touches than we think early on, but he likely fades into the background once Charbs returns from injury.
Dynasty: The 27-year-old signed a low-risk one-year deal worth up to $2.1 million. That contract and the drafting of Jadarian Price suggest he is nothing more than a depth piece for this squad.

Name: Isaiah Davis
Outlook: Davis is an explosive backup RB who averaged 5.5 yards per carry through two seasons, albeit on just 73 total attempts. He was also electric in the passing game, but averaged just 29% of the snaps behind Breece Hall. New OC Frank Reich and HC Aaron Glenn have toiled with the idea of a committee approach, but the contract extension and talent of Hall suggest otherwise. Expect a handful of touches per game but zero fantasy relevance to start the season.
Dynasty: The 24-year-old Davis enters Year 3 stuck behind Breece Hall, competing for bread crumbs alongside Braelon Allen. He sadly ended up on the wrong NFL team to make significant fantasy noise. For dynasty purposes, he is barely holding a bench spot, even in the deepest of leagues.

Name: Chris Brazzell II
Outlook: At 6’4” and 198 pounds, Brazzell has the prototypical perimeter deep threat build. He will likely compete with Xavier Legette for playing time as a rookie and be a part-time contributor. After all, are we really that excited about the third or fourth option in a Bryce Young offense? No offense, Bryce!
Dynasty: It is always tough to project these Tennessee WRs from college to the pros, so we see a wide range of outcomes for Brazzell. He is a tall, rangy perimeter player who can win down the field, but he likely needs to develop more of a refined skillset to make it in the league. He is a fine stash, but we are not overly excited about it.

Name: Roman Wilson
Outlook: Pittsburgh took Roman Wilson in the third round back in 2024, and after struggling to stay healthy in Year 1, he was just a part time player in 2025. Hey, to his credit, he did finish as the WR8 in Week 8 against the Packers, so he’s got that going for him. #NeverForget. He is likely to be a part-time player again in 2026, who is stuck behind Pitty City, Metcalf, and second-round rookie Germie Bernard.
Dynasty: There are hundreds of players you once took in the second or third round of a rookie draft who never went on to do anything in the league. Mr. Wilson is on that list, sadly. If he is still on your dynasty squad, take some advice from Michael Jordan: “Stop it. Get some help.”

Name: Kalif Raymond
Outlook: Raymond had a bit role with the Lions as an undersized slot receiver who primarily contributed on special teams. Ironically, he is now reunited with Ben Johnson in Chicago, so he should have no issues carving out a nice little role for the Bears. We do not expect that role to be fantasy relevant.

Perhaps he can carve out a role as the RB2 behind Etienne as the insurance back, but he is best left on waivers to start the year.

Ladd is the top guy here, while Oronde Gadsden and Quentin Johnston should see plenty of looks. Meanwhile, the team is reportedly high on last year’s second-round pick, Tre’ Harris. Name: Ollie Gordon II
Outlook: The 6’2” bruiser saw limited work as a rookie, averaging an utterly disgusting 2.8 yards per carry on 70 total carries. Sounds bad, right? Name: Marlin Klein
Outlook: The Texans really reached on Klein, grabbing the Michigan TE in the second round when most had a Day 3 grade on him. Texans, what do you know that we don’t?! Klein’s college production was lacking, but he did play behind one Mr. LOVEland, so maybe that is somewhat excusable… For redraft purposes, ignore and move on.
Dynasty: We DO have to pay attention to the draft capital, but that doesn’t mean we ignore a college production profile that looks like a dirty diaper. The Doctor is still in his residency for two more years (that is Dalton Schultz, btw), but past him, Klein just needs to bypass the likes of Foster Moreau and Cade Stover. Remember the name, but do not picture a future with Marlin.

Name: Audric Estimé
Outlook: After spending his rookie year in Denver, the one known as “Estime Lauder” found his way on the Saints roster and willed his way to two top-15 finishes in the final two weeks of the season. Before you get too excited, realize the entire Saints backfield depth chart was on injured reserve. Free agent signing Travis Etienne Jr. will be the bell-cow while Estimé occasionally mixes in for a handful of carries.
Dynasty: He is the definition of a BBB- a big-bodied back. However, unless your dynasty roster has a specific BBB slot, you can drop the underwhelming back.

Name: Kendrick Law
Outlook: Brad Holmes and Dan Campbell love to draft some dawgs with some grit. Hey, Law at least has that going for him. He is built like a RB and forced plenty of missed tackles in college. Unfortunately, the rookie tore his ACL in early June and will miss all of 2026.
Dynasty: Law is a really weak prospect from an analytical perspective, and now he is buried behind legit studs. The only reason you should be looking at Law in dynasty is if you participate in eight-round rookie drafts, and those don’t exist.

Name: Nate Boerkircher
Outlook: Boerkircher (pronounced BOAR-kur-chur) was a surprise second-round selection by the Jaguars. The draft capital is enough to make you pay attention, but when you look back at his performance in college, you are left wanting more - he never topped more than 22 targets in college. Just called him “Boerkircher the Blocker” as that will probably be his main task in Jacksonville.
Dynasty: You have to at least pay attention to the name with Brenton Strange, Quintin Morris, and Hunter Long all due to be free agents come 2027. The Jaguars' TE cupboard will be practically bare. However, the team also drafted Tanner Koziol, who profiles more as the pass-catching TE, suggesting Boerkircher will have to push to be considered in 12 and 13 personnel sets.

Name: Oscar Delp
Outlook: There was a lot of chatter around what could have been with Oscar Delp, but looking at his numbers from college, he never really delivered. He never hit 300 receiving yards at Georgia thanks to competition with some guy named Brock Bowers basically running the offense during Delp’s first two seasons. Even with questions on the production profile, the Saints grabbed him in the third round to add youth to their TE room. He will have a tough time making an impact in redraft this year, presumably buried on the TE depth chart behind Juwan Johnson and Noah Fant.
Dynasty: The athleticism is there, and Delp has the experience in a good college system. That plus draft capital should put him on your dynasty radar as a classic wait-and-see stash. Delp should be taken in your rookie drafts - probably around the TE4/TE5 - and you can sit back and wait to see what Kellen Moore can scheme for the man.
